solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2219D - MEX Replacement on Tree
Root out the simple case:
It's obvious that applying the operation on $$$v$$$ where $$$p_v  \lt  f(v)$$$ is useless, since it would decrease $$$f(x)$$$ for every $$$x$$$ in $$$v$$$'s subtree.
So from now on, we assume $$$p_v  \gt  f(v)$$$.
Rephrase of operation (kind of) :
To understand how the $$$f(x)$$$ would change, I would like to introduce an intuitive way to think about the $$$\mathrm{MEX}$$$ function first.
Given a set of integers, imagine there is an array with infinite empty slots, indexed from $$$0$$$. Then for each element $$$\mathrm{val}$$$ in the set, we fill the $$$\mathrm{val}$$$-th slot with a token, and then we can think of the $$$\mathrm{MEX}$$$ function as finding the first hole starting from the $$$0$$$-th slot.
Then you can imagine there is an array $$$a_x$$$ with infinite slots for each vertex $$$x$$$ containing all elements on the path from $$$1$$$ to $$$x$$$, and think of the operation as picking up the token at the $$$p_v$$$-th slot and moving it to the first hole of $$$a_v$$$(that is, $$$f(v)$$$) for each array $$$a_x$$$ where $$$x$$$ is in $$$v$$$'s subtree.
Further analysis:
After doing such a rephrase and playing with it a bit on paper, you would probably notice there are a few cases:
for $$$a_x$$$ with its $$$f(v)$$$-th slot being empty, moving a token from $$$p_v$$$ to $$$f(v)$$$ would fill the first hole and create a hole at $$$f(v)$$$, so the new first hole would be the second hole of the original $$$a_x$$$ if it is less than $$$p_v$$$ or $$$p_v$$$ otherwise.
for $$$a_x$$$ with its $$$f(v)$$$-th slot having a token in it, moving a token from $$$p_v$$$ to $$$f(v)$$$ would make the $$$f(v)$$$-th slot have $$$2$$$ tokens and create a hole at $$$f(v)$$$, so the new first hole would remain unchanged if $$$f(x)  \lt  p_v$$$ or $$$p_v$$$ otherwise.
So in conclusion, for the first case $$$f(x)$$$ would become $$$\mathrm{min}(g(x), p_v)$$$ after doing operation on $$$v$$$, where $$$g(x)$$$ is almost the same as $$$f(x)$$$ but it finds second $$$\mathrm{MEX}$$$. And for the second case $$$f(x)$$$ would become $$$\mathrm{min}(f(x), p_v)$$$ after doing operation on $$$v$$$.
Then we can consider processing $$$v$$$ in decreasing order of $$$p_v$$$, and maintain
the sum of $$$g(x)$$$ for $$$g(x)  \lt  p_v$$$
the number of vertices with $$$g(x) \ge p_v$$$
the sum of $$$f(x)$$$ for $$$f(x)  \lt  p_v$$$
the number of vertices with $$$f(x) \ge p_v$$$
under every subtree in $$$O(n\lg n)$$$. Which is a standard [Vertex Add Subtree Sum Problem](https://judge.yosupo.jp/problem/vertex_add_subtree_sum) and can be solved by fenwick tree + euler tour trick.
For the query part, case $$$1$$$ is just querying for data $$$1, 2$$$ for $$$v$$$'s subtree; case $$$2$$$ would happen if and only if there exists some vertex $$$x$$$ with $$$p_x = f(v)$$$ under $$$v$$$'s subtree, then we just change the contribution of $$$x$$$'s subtree from data $$$1, 2$$$ to data $$$3, 4$$$. And we can check whether $$$v$$$ is $$$x$$$'s ancestor using the time stamp of two vertices.
